name: Bull-5
classification_notes: Derived from 1960s typewriter fonts attributed to Olivetti, with Mono, Proportional and Typewriter subfamilies that all retain the mechanical irregularities of the source material.
summary: A typewriter revival by David Einwaller, digitised from 1960s Olivetti-derived type specimens and extended across three stylistic subfamilies.
description: Drawn from photographic print labels sourced from a closing American newspaper archive, Bull-5 preserves the irregular proportions and raw rhythm of its typewriter source material across Mono, Proportional and Typewriter styles. Bold punctuation and optional features including double-width M and W and serifed I, i, J and j run through all variants as a unifying thread. Six weights from Regular to Black, each with an italic, give it a range unusual for a typewriter-derived face.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describes Bull-5 as deriving from a particular style of typewriter fonts that emerged in the 1960s, likely through Olivetti. Designer David Einwaller began by digitising printed type samples found on labels of photographic prints from a closing American newspaper archive, preserving the source's quirks and irregular proportions in the Mono styles, while adding Proportional styles for legibility and Typewriter styles to honour another characteristic of the genre.

name: Bull-5 Mono
classification_notes: Bull-5 Mono is explicitly a monospaced family derived from a 1960s typewriter font style, likely originating with Olivetti, digitised from printed photographic print labels and drawn to preserve mechanical irregularity.
summary: A monospaced family digitised from printed type samples on labels from a defunct American newspaper archive, tracing a 1960s Olivetti typewriter style with all its mechanical quirks intact.
description: David Einwaller began the project by scanning photographic print labels sold at the closure of an American newspaper archive, then drew the Mono styles to honour the source material's irregular proportions and raw character. The family sits within the broader Bull-5 system alongside Proportional and Typewriter styles, unified by bold punctuation and optional features including double-width M and W and serifed I, i, J and j. Six weights from Regular to Black give considerable range for editorial, archival and design-in-industry contexts.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Bull-5 Mono as derived from a 1960s typewriter font style presumably introduced through Olivetti. The project started by digitising printed type samples found on labels from photographic prints sold at the closure of an American newspaper archive. The Mono styles are drawn close to the source material, preserving its mechanical character and irregular proportions, and are accompanied by Proportional styles for legibility and Typewriter styles to reference another iconic quality of typewriter typography. All styles are unified through bold punctuation and optional features such as double-width M and W and serifed I, i, J and j.

name: Bull-5 Typewriter
classification_notes: Derived from 1960s Olivetti typewriter typefaces, the Typewriter subfamily embraces the fixed-pitch, mechanical character of classic typewriter fonts, making mono the appropriate category.
summary: One of three subfamilies in the Bull-5 system, the Typewriter cut foregrounds the fixed-pitch eccentricities of 1960s Olivetti-era office typography, drawn from labels on photographic prints recovered from an American newspaper archive.
description: Digitised from printed type samples found on photo-print labels acquired at a newspaper archive closure, the Typewriter styles carry the irregular proportions and mechanical quirks of their source material intact. Six weights run from Regular to Black, sharing bold punctuation and optional features including double-width M and W and serifed I, i, J and j with the Mono and Proportional subfamilies. The result is a typewriter revival with archival specificity rather than generic nostalgia.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Bull-5 as deriving from a particular style of typewriter fonts that emerged in the 1960s, presumably introduced through Olivetti. The project began by digitising printed samples from photo-print labels sold at the closure of an American newspaper archive. The Typewriter styles embrace an iconic characteristic of typewriter fonts, accompanied by Mono styles faithful to the quirky source material and Proportional styles for greater legibility, with all three linked through bold punctuation and shared optional features.

name: Exposit
classification_notes: Derived from Joseph A. David's 1876 stencil system 'Plaque Découpée Universelle', Exposit is a display typeface with structural stencil logic and variable letterform expression driven by amplitude, width and stretch parameters.
summary: A stencil display typeface developed from Joseph A. David's 1876 grid-based lettering system, with interchangeable amplitude, width and stretch settings that shift the tone from calm to turbulent.
description: Built on the logic of David's 'Plaque Découpée Universelle', Exposit treats its stencil origins as a live design variable rather than a fixed aesthetic. Stroke lengths, letter widths and rounded forms can be adjusted to produce uniform, restrained setting or something far more expressive. The family runs across five weights plus contrasted Positive and Negative styles, backed by an extensive ligature set and three stylistic sets that offer further structural alternatives.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Exposit as a reinterpretation of Joseph A. David's 1876 stencil system, which allowed all letters, numbers and symbols to be drawn from a single grid. The typeface uses interplay between vertical stroke lengths (Amplitude), narrow and wide letters (Widths) and expanding rounded shapes (Stretch) to produce letterforms that can read as uniform and calm or irregular and turbulent. It is available in five weights alongside Positive and Negative contrasted styles, with a comprehensive ligature set and OpenType features.

name: Fleya
classification_notes: Described as a modern serif with a classicist approach, blending contemporary type design with details evoking metal typesetting irregularities, including filled narrow gaps and calligraphic ascenders and descenders.
summary: A contemporary serif drawing on classicist proportions and the incidental character of metal typesetting, with calligraphic ascenders, filled narrow gaps and a companion mono subfamily.
description: Fleya runs across four weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic, plus four Mono styles that make it an unusually complete family for a smaller foundry. Distinctive details include calligraphic ascenders and descenders and filled narrow gaps that recall the ink traps and quirks of metal composition. The character set covers a wide range of Latin-extended languages and the OpenType feature set includes multiple numeral styles, extensive ligatures and three stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Fleya as a modern serif typeface with a classicist approach, mixing contemporary type design with unintended effects of metal typesetting. Particular details such as filled narrow gaps and calligraphic ascenders and descenders give the typeface its distinctive flair, and the family includes a wide range of OpenType features, four weights with corresponding italics and four Mono styles.

name: Habitas
classification_notes: A sans serif with geometric and classical influences, drawing from Roman and Greek architectural forms rather than a purely industrial or humanist tradition. The column-like lowercase 'i' and upright italic bars give it a distinctive structural character.
summary: A sans serif family of 20 styles whose forms draw from Roman and Greek architectural severity, most distinctively expressed through a column-shaped lowercase 'i' carried into its unusual upright-bar italics.
description: The column-like lowercase 'i' acts as the structural motif for the whole family, echoed across other characters and most notably in the italic, where horizontal bars remain upright rather than following the angle of inclination. Ten weights run from Hairline to Super, each with a matching italic, giving considerable range for editorial or branding work. Language support covers major Western European Latin scripts, with a thorough set of OpenType features including stylistic alternates for several key characters.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Habitas as a sans serif family influenced by the severe aesthetics of Roman and Greek architecture. The lowercase 'i', reminiscent of ancient columns, anchors the design concept and is reflected across other letters and symbols. Unlike traditional italics, horizontal bars in Habitas end upright, creating a deliberate conflict with the underlying angle that reinforces the column motif.

name: Lemur
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans-serif; the even, constructed letterforms and circular stroke endings are consistent with the geometric tradition.
summary: A seven-weight geometric sans covering an extended Latin character set with a broad range of OpenType features.
description: Spanning Extra Light through Extra Bold, it offers a clean geometric construction suited to editorial, branding and screen work. The character set includes extended Latin diacritics for over two dozen languages, with OpenType support for stylistic alternates, tabular and proportional figures, fractions, superscripts and subscripts. No italic or condensed cuts are available, keeping the family focused and lean.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Lemur as a geometric sans-serif typeface available across seven styles, from Extra Light to Extra Bold, with a comprehensive character set and language support for a wide range of Latin-based languages.

name: Macan
classification_notes: Macan draws directly from 20th-century Grotesque sources (Gothic No. 124, Times Gothic, Modern Gothic) and positions itself as a Neo-Grotesque; its stencil companion adds ornamental bridges inspired by rave-era type but the core family is firmly within the grotesque sans tradition.
summary: A Neo-Grotesque family in nine weights with a matching stencil companion, drawing on 20th-century grotesque sources and '90s acid house flyer aesthetics to produce something with genuine personality.
description: Rooted in Gothic No. 124, Times Gothic and Modern Gothic, Macan delivers a clean Neo-Grotesque base with a wide weight range and extensive OpenType features including a large set of discretionary ligatures and 15 stylistic sets. Its Stencil companion reframes the functional bridge of traditional stencil letterforms as decorative ornament, taking cues from Keedy Sans and the whimsical type found on Wicked Sound System promotional material. Both subfamilies share identical character widths, making them directly interchangeable, and the collection extends to Hebrew and Thai for broad multilingual use.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Macan as a blend of 20th-century Grotesque influences, stencil typeface aesthetics and the energy of '90s rave flyers, mixed into a versatile Neo-Grotesque family. The Stencil version is said to treat its connector bridges as ornament rather than pure function, inspired by Keedy Sans as spotted on flyers from San Francisco's Acid House scene. Both families were developed in parallel so that their rhythm, proportions and character widths align perfectly, enabling seamless pairing.

name: Macan Mono
classification_notes: A mono-spaced neo-grotesque drawing on 20th-century Grotesque references (Gothic No. 124, Times Gothic, Modern Gothic), paired with a stencil companion that introduces ornamental bridges rooted in early-90s rave typography.
summary: A monospaced neo-grotesque with a stencil companion, drawing on 20th-century Grotesque models and 1990s rave flyer typography.
description: Nine weights without italics form the core family, with a matching Stencil sub-family whose rounded and straight bridges treat the functional connectors of stencil cutting as decorative elements rather than structural necessities. Both sub-families share identical character widths, making them directly interchangeable within a layout. Coverage extends across Latin (including Vietnamese), Cyrillic and Greek, with a thorough set of OpenType features including contextual alternates, multiple stylistic sets and comprehensive figure options.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Macan Mono as blending the Grotesque genre, industrial stencil aesthetics and the visual language of 90s rave flyers into a versatile neo-grotesque family. The stencil variant takes inspiration from Keedy Sans as seen on Wicked Sound System flyers, treating bridges as both functional and ornamental elements. Macan and Macan Stencil were developed in parallel, sharing character widths so the two sub-families work together interchangeably.

name: Moderat
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans-serif with low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details — hallmarks of the geometric sans genre with a cooler, more structured character.
summary: A geometric sans with closed apertures and angular detailing, available across three widths and nine weights with matching italics.
description: Designed in 2015, Moderat works across a substantial 21-style superfamily spanning Regular, Condensed and Extended widths. Low stroke contrast and closed apertures give it a crisp, contained character that suits both display and text use at reasonable sizes. An extensive OpenType feature set includes multiple stylistic sets, case-sensitive forms, fractions and a slashed zero, backed by coverage across Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic scripts including Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Moderat as a geometric sans-serif designed in 2015 with low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details as its defining characteristics. It is available in three widths — Regular, Condensed and Extended — across nine weights with matching italics, and includes an enhanced set of OpenType features and stylistic alternates.

name: Moderat Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of Moderat, a geometric sans-serif with low stroke contrast and closed apertures; the mono classification follows directly from its fixed-width construction within the Moderat family.
summary: A monospaced companion to the Moderat geometric sans, available in nine weights with matching italics and broad Latin, Greek and Cyrillic coverage.
description: Sharing the low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details of the parent Moderat family, this fixed-width variant runs from Thin to Black across 18 styles. The character set is extensive, covering Vietnamese, Greek and Cyrillic in addition to a wide range of Western Latin languages, and OpenType features include fractions, superscript, subscript, slashed zero and multiple stylistic sets for alternates on characters such as a, g and l.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describes Moderat as a geometric sans-serif designed in 2015, defined by low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details. The family spans three widths and nine weights with italics throughout, and Moderat Mono brings that same character to a fixed-width format with an enhanced set of OpenType features and stylistic alternates.

name: Moderat Rounded
classification_notes: Moderat is explicitly described as a geometric sans-serif with low stroke contrast and closed apertures; the Rounded variant softens the angular terminals while retaining the geometric skeleton.
summary: A rounded variant of the geometric sans Moderat, available in ten weights with matching italics and broad Latin, Greek and Cyrillic coverage.
description: Built on the same low-contrast, closed-aperture skeleton as Moderat, this variant applies softened terminals across ten weights from Thin to Black, each with a corresponding italic. The family covers Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic scripts alongside a thorough set of OpenType features including multiple stylistic sets, fractions, tabular figures and a slashed zero. Related families Moderat Mono and Moderat Serif sit alongside it in the wider Moderat system.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Moderat as a geometric sans-serif designed in 2015, characterised by low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details. The Rounded family offers 20 styles across ten weights with italics, enhanced OpenType features and stylistic alternates, and is available in three widths.

name: Moderat Serif
classification_notes: Described as a serif companion to Moderat, a geometric typeface family; it carries low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details consistent with a geometric serif classification.
summary: A geometric serif companion to Moderat, carrying the same low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details across nine weights with italics.
description: Built to sit alongside Tightype's Moderat sans, this serif shares its geometric structure and angular character details, making it a natural pairing within the same design system. Nine weights from Thin to Black each have a matching italic, giving twenty styles in total. An enhanced OpenType feature set includes small capitals, swashes, multiple figure styles, stylistic alternates for a, g and y, and broad Latin coverage extending to Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Moderat Serif as the serif companion to Moderat, a geometric typeface family defined by low stroke contrast, closed apertures and angular details. The family spans nine weights with corresponding italics and offers an enhanced set of OpenType features and stylistic alternates for versatility.

name: Peclet
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a high-contrast sans serif with roots in nineteenth-century grotesques. The combination of strong stroke contrast with a grotesque structure places it firmly in that subcategory.
summary: A high-contrast grotesque with nineteenth-century roots, distinguished by unusually low baseline positioning and subtly eccentric terminals across fourteen styles.
description: Short descenders and long ascenders push the baseline uncomfortably low, giving the heavier weights a quality the foundry describes as strong digital gravity. Repetitive minor angles and slightly disproportionate terminals introduce eccentricity without breaking the typeface's underlying rigidity. The family runs from Thin to Black with corresponding italics, covering a broad range of Latin Extended languages including Central and Eastern European scripts.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Peclet as a high-contrast sans serif rooted in nineteenth-century grotesques. Its short descenders and long ascenders shift the baseline uncommonly low, creating a sense of digital gravity most pronounced in the heavier weights. Minor angles and slightly disproportionate terminals add eccentricity to an otherwise rigid structure. The family comprises fourteen styles from Thin to Black with matching italics.

name: Plaid
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans with dual variable axes (weight and width), ranging from extra-small and narrow to extra-large and wide, suggesting a neo-grotesque lineage with structural flexibility.
summary: A grotesque sans spanning five width-and-weight steps from extra-small narrow to extra-large wide, with matching italics and a monospaced companion.
description: Plaid operates across two simultaneous axes, weight and width, offering five named steps from XS to XL with corresponding italics, and a five-style Mono subfamily. The result is a family that can appear restrained or commanding depending on which end of the range is called upon. Extended Latin and Cyrillic script support is included, alongside a full suite of OpenType features covering stylistic sets, fractions and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Plaid as moving from light and narrow to bold and wide, touching extremes via its combined weight and width axes. The typeface is available in XS, S, M, L and XL sizes with corresponding italics and mono styles, supported by extended language coverage including Cyrillic and a wide range of OpenType features.

name: Sneak
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque, Sneak follows the upright, rational structure typical of the genre but introduces reversed characters — most notably the S — as a deliberate typographic quirk.
summary: A neo-grotesque with a defining gimmick: several characters, including the S, are drawn in mirror reversal.
description: Available in five weights with matching italics plus a single Mono style, the family covers an extended Latin character set with broad diacritic support across roughly 28 languages. Multiple stylistic sets offer alternate forms for characters such as the double-storey a, dotted punctuation and the standard versus reversed figures. The reversed letterforms give it a point of difference suited to branding or editorial work where a familiar grotesque structure with an unexpected detail is the brief.
foundry_blurb: Tightype describe Sneak as a neo-grotesque typeface featuring several reversed characters, with the distinctive mirrored S as its centrepiece. The family ships in five weights with corresponding italics and a monospaced style, totalling eleven styles.
